summary:
Canto LV

King Nimi and sage Vasistha leave their bodies as a
result of mutual curse
content: King Nimi, a descendant of Iksvaku, built a city named Vaijayanta and decided to perform a long sacrifice to please his father.  He first requested Vasistha, a revered sage, to be the chief priest. Vasistha informed Nimi he was already committed to Indra's sacrifice and asked him to wait.  Nimi then proceeded with the sacrifice, appointing Gautama as the chief priest.  After completing Indra's sacrifice, Vasistha returned and was angered to find Gautama officiating in his promised role.

summary:
Canto LV

King Nimi and sage Vasistha leave their bodies as a
result of mutual curse
content: Vasistha waited for King Nimi, but the king was asleep.  Unable to see Nimi, Vasistha became angry and cursed the king, saying that since Nimi had chosen another priest while disregarding him, the king's body would become lifeless. Upon awakening and learning of the curse, Nimi, filled with anger, retorted that Vasistha had unfairly cursed him while he was asleep and unaware.  Nimi, in turn, cursed Vasistha to also become disembodied. Consequently, both the king and the sage immediately lost their physical bodies.

summary:
Canto LVI
At the instance of Brahma Vasistha enters the semen of Varuna;
Varuna places his semen in a pitcher near Urvast; as
a consequence of Mitra’s curse Urvasi lives
with king Purtrava on the earth
and bears him a son
content: Upon hearing Rama speak, Lakshmana, a formidable warrior, respectfully inquired about the reincarnation of a Brahmin and a king, both honored by the gods.  Rama, descendant of Ikshvaku, explained that the Brahmin and king, who had cursed each other, became formless after death. The majestic Vasistha, now without a physical body, went to his father, Brahma, for guidance.  Vasistha explained his predicament, emphasizing the limitations of a formless existence. Brahma instructed Vasistha to enter the combined essence of Mitra and Varuna, promising a non-womb birth and a return to Brahma's domain.  Vasistha, following Brahma's instructions, departed for Varuna's abode.  At that time, Mitra shared Varuna's position, residing in the celestial sea of milk and receiving homage from the gods.  Coincidentally, the beautiful celestial nymph Urvasi arrived there with her companions.  Varuna, captivated by Urvasi's presence and beauty, was filled with joy.

summary:
Canto LVI
At the instance of Brahma Vasistha enters the semen of Varuna;
Varuna places his semen in a pitcher near Urvast; as
a consequence of Mitra’s curse Urvasi lives
with king Purtrava on the earth
and bears him a son
content: Varuna, captivated by Urvasi's beauty, proposed to her. Urvasi politely declined, explaining that she was already committed to Mitra.  Overcome with desire, Varuna released his semen into a divine pitcher.  Urvasi, though fond of Varuna, reiterated her commitment to Mitra and departed. Upon her return, a furious Mitra cursed Urvasi to live amongst mortals, specifically with King Pururava of Kasi.  As a result of this union, a powerful son named Ayu was born, whose own son, Nahusa, possessed the splendor of Indra.

summary:
Canto LVI
At the instance of Brahma Vasistha enters the semen of Varuna;
Varuna places his semen in a pitcher near Urvast; as
a consequence of Mitra’s curse Urvasi lives
with king Purtrava on the earth
and bears him a son
content: Nahusa, Ayu's son, ruled the heavens for an impressive hundred thousand years while Indra's position was vacant.  Meanwhile, due to Mitra's curse, the beautiful Urvasi resided on Earth for many years.  After the duration of her curse ended, she returned to the celestial realm.

summary:
Canto LVII
Sage Vasistha is re-incarnated and king Nimi resides in the
eye-lids of living beings
content: Lakshmana, delighted by the story, asks Rama how the revered Brahmin Vasistha and King Nimi were reborn. Rama explains that Vasistha and another Brahmin were born from a pitcher containing the combined essence of Mitra and Varuna.  The first born, Agastya, declared he was not solely Mitra's son and departed. Vasistha, born later from the combined essence, became the Ikshvaku family priest.  Rama then promises to tell Nimi’s story.  After King Videha's death, sages performed a sacrifice, preserving his body. Bhrigu, pleased with the king, then offered to restore his consciousness.

summary:
Canto LVII
Sage Vasistha is re-incarnated and king Nimi resides in the
eye-lids of living beings
content: The gods, pleased with King Nimi, offered him a boon. Nimi chose to reside on the eyelids of all living beings.  The gods granted his wish, decreeing that he would exist there as air, and that living beings would blink to give him rest.  After the gods departed, the sages performed a ritual with Nimi's body, hoping to produce an heir. From this ritual, a son was born. He was known by several names: Mithi (born from friction), Videha (born from the body of King Videha), and Janaka (born mysteriously).  This Janaka became the first of the Maithila dynasty.  Thus, Rama concludes the story, explaining the births of Vasistha and Nimi, both resulting from a series of curses between the king and the Brahmin.

Yayati's wives, Sarmistha and Devayani, bore him sons. Sarmistha's son, Puru, was favored by Yayati, causing grief to Devayani and her son, Yadu. Yadu, distressed by his mother's sorrow and perceived humiliation, proposed they either enter fire together or he be allowed to leave, as he could not bear the situation.

Devayani, deeply distressed by her son Yadu's anguish, called upon her father, Shukracharya.  Shukracharya, noticing his daughter's sorrow, questioned her about its cause. Devayani, overwhelmed with anger and grief, declared her intention to end her life, explaining to her father that she felt deeply humiliated by Yayati's disregard for her.  She emphasized the severity of the insult, comparing it to the careless destruction of a tree.  Devayani then revealed that Yayati, the king, ignored and undervalued her.  Enraged by his daughter's distress, Shukracharya cursed Yayati to prematurely lose his strength and youth due to old age. Having pronounced this curse and comforted Devayani, Shukracharya returned to his own dwelling.

summary:
Canto LIX
King Yayati borrows youth from his son Puru, in exchange for his own old
age. Satisfied after a long time with the enjoyment of the objects
of pleasure, he returns his youth to Puru and consecrates
him king and pronounces curse on Yadu
content: King Yayati, weighed down by old age, asks his son Yadu to exchange his youth for his father's old age.  Upon hearing Rama, Lakshmana quickly brought a dog before him. Rama urged the dog to speak without fear. The dog, addressing Rama, described the ideal king as the creator, leader, and protector of his people, emphasizing the king's responsibility to uphold dharma, the moral order. Rama then instructed Lakshmana to summon those who had come with requests.  Lakshmana reported back that no one had any grievances. The dog continued, explaining that dharma sustains the world and that a righteous king protects even his enemies.  Under Rama's rule, the land prospered, and no one suffered illness, premature death, or unmet needs.

summary:
Canto LIX
King Yayati borrows youth from his son Puru, in exchange for his own old
age. Satisfied after a long time with the enjoyment of the objects
of pleasure, he returns his youth to Puru and consecrates
him king and pronounces curse on Yadu
content: A dog appealed to Rama for justice, claiming a mendicant named Sarvarthasiddha had struck him without cause.  The dog extolled Rama as an ideal king, a refuge for all beings, and the embodiment of dharma. He stressed that a king should acquire and protect his kingdom through righteousness.  Rama, wanting to address the situation fairly, summoned the mendicant.  The mendicant, upon seeing Rama, inquired how he could be of service.  Rama confronted the mendicant about striking the dog and admonished him about the destructive nature of anger, which could nullify even acts of charity and sacrifice. He advised the mendicant to control his anger, comparing unruly senses to wild horses that must be reigned in by a charioteer.  Lakshmana, having overheard the dog’s complaint, relayed it to Rama and subsequently returned to inform the dog that his grievance had reached the king.

summary:
Canto LIX
King Yayati borrows youth from his son Puru, in exchange for his own old
age. Satisfied after a long time with the enjoyment of the objects
of pleasure, he returns his youth to Puru and consecrates
him king and pronounces curse on Yadu
content: The dog, having heard Lakshmana’s words, expressed his reluctance to enter the royal palace where deities like Agni, Indra, Surya, and Vayu were present. He felt unworthy due to his lowly status. He described Rama as the embodiment of dharma, a truthful and skilled warrior dedicated to the welfare of all beings. The dog extolled Rama's wisdom, justice, and omniscience, comparing him to various gods. He further emphasized the importance of good deeds in thought, word, and action.  He then requested Lakshmana to inform Rama of his presence and seek permission to enter, as he did not wish to do so uninvited. Lakshmana, moved by the dog's humility, relayed the message to Rama within the palace. Rama, upon hearing the dog's request, immediately commanded Lakshmana to bring the dog inside.

summary:
Canto LIX
King Yayati borrows youth from his son Puru, in exchange for his own old
age. Satisfied after a long time with the enjoyment of the objects
of pleasure, he returns his youth to Puru and consecrates
him king and pronounces curse on Yadu
content: A Brahmin, Sarvarthasiddha, confessed to Rama that he struck a dog out of anger and hunger when it blocked his path while he was begging for food. He asked Rama to punish him for this transgression.  Rama consulted his assembly, including learned sages and ministers, about the appropriate punishment. The sages declared Rama, as Vishnu, the ruler of the three worlds and the ultimate authority. The dog then spoke, requesting that the Brahmin be appointed Kulapati (head of a monastery) of Kalanjara. Rama granted this request.  The ministers questioned this decision, thinking it a boon rather than punishment. Rama explained they didn't understand the situation’s full implications, which the dog would reveal. The dog recounted its past life as the Kulapati of Kalanjara, devoted to piety and good deeds, yet reborn as a dog. It explained that the Brahmin, driven by anger and abandoning dharma, would cause the downfall of fourteen generations. It argued against appointing anyone as Kulapati who might misuse their position, urging instead to entrust such individuals with the care of gods, cows, and Brahmins.  The dog warned against misappropriating the property of Brahmins, gods, women, or children, emphasizing the severe karmic consequences. Amazed by the dog's wisdom, Rama watched it depart. The dog, a noble soul in its previous life but tainted by its current form, sought death through fasting in Varanasi.  Rama had initially consulted his assembly, including prominent sages and legal experts, who advised against capital punishment for a Brahmin.

The gods and ascetics, tormented and fearing for their lives at the hands of the Rakshasas, sought refuge in Lord Shiva. They described their plight to the three-eyed deity, explaining how the sons of Sukesa, empowered by boons, were harassing all beings and making their homes uninhabitable. The Rakshasas, including Mali, Sumali, and Malyavan, boasted of being equal to the gods, causing further fear.  The gods pleaded with Lord Shiva to protect them and destroy these adversaries.  Lord Shiva, acknowledging his limitations in directly confronting the Rakshasas due to boons they had received, advised the gods to seek the help of Lord Vishnu, assuring them that Vishnu would be the one to ultimately vanquish these foes.  Heartened by this advice, the gods departed for Vishnu's abode, praising him with reverence and hope. They reported to Vishnu how the sons of Sukesa had seized their rightful places, highlighting the urgency of the situation.

The gods reported to Vishnu that the Rakshasas, dwelling in the inaccessible city of Lanka atop Mount Trikuta, were tormenting them. They implored Vishnu, as their protector, to eliminate these foes.  The gods declared that only Vishnu could offer them refuge from this terror, requesting him to slay the arrogant Rakshasas and deliver them to Yama, the god of death.  Vishnu, acknowledging their plea, assured the gods that he knew of Sukesa and his sons, including the eldest, Malyavan, who had grown arrogant due to boons received from Shiva. He promised to destroy these Rakshasas who had transgressed all boundaries. Reassured, the gods departed, praising Vishnu. Malyavan, informed of the gods' meeting with Vishnu, addressed his brothers, recounting how the gods and seers had complained to Shiva about the sons of Sukesa, who, empowered by boons, constantly tormented them and prevented them from residing peacefully in their homes.  The gods had beseeched Shiva to destroy these Rakshasas for the well-being of all.

Malyavan, aware of the gods' plea to Vishnu, warned his brothers about the impending danger. He reminded them that Vishnu had vowed to destroy them.  Malyavan urged his brothers to consider their precarious situation, highlighting Vishnu’s power by recounting how Vishnu had vanquished formidable foes like Hiranyakashipu, Namuci, Kalanemi, and others.  These foes, despite their mastery of sacrifices, magic, weaponry, and battle prowess, were ultimately defeated by Vishnu. Malyavan stressed the difficulty of overcoming Vishnu, who was intent on their destruction. Following this warning, Sumali and Mali addressed their elder brother Malyavan, asserting their accomplishments: their Vedic studies, charitable acts, sacrifices, accumulated wealth, and long healthy lives rooted in righteousness.  They boasted of their prowess, claiming to have conquered even the gods and declared themselves fearless in the face of death.  They even proclaimed that powerful deities like Vishnu, Shiva, Indra, and Yama were afraid of them.

Malyavan's brothers, Sumali and Mali, dismissed his warnings, boasting of their piety, power, and accomplishments.  They claimed to have conquered even the gods and declared themselves unafraid of death, asserting that even powerful deities feared them.  Driven by their arrogance, they rallied their forces to attack the gods. The Raksasa army, including formidable leaders like Jambha and Vrtra, marched towards the gods’ realm, boasting of their strength and accompanied by various creatures. Ominous signs, including unusual weather phenomena and the strange behavior of animals, foreshadowed the Raksasas' impending doom.  However, blinded by their arrogance, the Raksasas ignored these warnings and continued their march. Malyavan, Sumali, and Mali led the charge, their power likened to blazing fires. Meanwhile, Vishnu, hearing of the Raksasas' plans, prepared for battle. Adorned in celestial armor and armed with his divine weapons, he mounted Garuda, ready to confront the Raksasas. Vishnu's appearance was radiant and majestic, likened to a lightning-streaked cloud on Mount Meru. Accompanied by the praises of celestial beings, he arrived at the battlefield, ready to vanquish his foes.  The mere flapping of Garuda's wings caused disarray among the Raksasa army, scattering their weapons and banners.
Vishnu, facing the Raksasa army, was surrounded and attacked by thousands of them. Their weapons, sharp and covered in blood and flesh, were likened to the destructive fires of the apocalypse.

summary:
Canto LX
Sages, living on the bank of Yamuna, terrorised by Asura Lavana approach

Sri Rama, in order to make a complaint. Sri Rama receives them
with honour and enquires about the cause of their arrival
content: As Rama and Lakshmana conversed, a pleasant vernal night arrived. The following morning, after completing his rituals, Rama went to meet his subjects.  Sumantra, his charioteer, informed him that ascetics, led by the sage Cyavana, were waiting at the entrance, requesting an audience. Rama immediately welcomed the sages, including Cyavana, into his palace. The ascetics, radiating an aura of holiness, presented Rama with pitchers of sacred water from various pilgrimage sites and offered him fruits and roots.  Rama graciously accepted the offerings.  He then offered the sages seats of honor and inquired about the purpose of their visit, assuring them of his assistance and dedication to their welfare, even offering his kingdom and life in their service. Deeply moved by Rama's words, the sages praised his devotion and acknowledged that such commitment was unique to him.  They explained that many powerful kings in the past had been unable to fulfill their request due to the magnitude of the task, but Rama, out of respect for the Brahmins, made the promise without hesitation.  They expressed their confidence that Rama would indeed alleviate their fears.

summary:
Canto LXI

The sages inform Sri Rama about the boon obtained by the Asura, Madhu,
and the might, and atrocities perpetrated by Lavanasura and
appeal to Sri Rama to dispel their fear
content: The sages informed Rama about the Asura Madhu, detailing his boon and the resulting fear he instilled.  Rama urged them to elaborate on the task he should perform to alleviate their fear.  Bhargava, one of the sages, began to narrate the story of Madhu, the eldest son of Lola, from the Krtayuga. He described Madhu as respectful to Brahmins, a source of refuge, and possessing a cordial relationship with the gods. Though powerful, Madhu was righteous. Due to his respect for the Devas, he was granted a boon by Lord Shiva in the form of a powerful, luminous trident.  Shiva explained that the trident’s power would remain as long as Madhu did not oppose Brahmins and gods.  The trident was capable of reducing any fearless opponent to ashes and returning to Madhu’s hand.  Upon receiving the boon, Madhu requested that the trident remain within his lineage forever. However, Shiva stated that this was not possible.

summary:
Canto LXI

The sages inform Sri Rama about the boon obtained by the Asura, Madhu,
and the might, and atrocities perpetrated by Lavanasura and
appeal to Sri Rama to dispel their fear
content: The sages continued their narration, explaining that Shiva's boon declared Madhu's son invincible as long as he possessed the trident.  Madhu, after receiving this boon, built a magnificent palace. His wife, Kumbhinasi, bore a son named Lavana.  From childhood, Lavana exhibited wickedness, performing only evil deeds. Though grieved and angered by his son's behavior, Madhu remained silent.  Before leaving the world to enter the ocean, Madhu entrusted Lavana with the powerful trident and the knowledge of its boon.  Empowered by the trident and driven by his inherent wickedness, Lavana terrorized the three worlds, particularly targeting ascetics. The sages emphasized Lavana's might and the trident's power, imploring Rama, their last hope, for protection.  They recounted how previous kings had failed to protect them from Lavana.  With Ravana vanquished, they saw Rama as their sole protector from Lavana’s tyranny, beseeching him to alleviate their fear and remove its cause.

summary:
Canto LXII
Rama enquires of the sages regarding Lavana’s way of living and dispatches
Satrughna with a view to kill Lavana
content: The sages informed Rama about Lavana's habits: he consumed all beings, especially ascetics, behaved cruelly, and resided in Madhuvana.  Lavana killed countless lions, tigers, deer, birds, and humans for his daily meals, devouring them like Death itself. Upon hearing this, Rama assured the sages that he would eliminate Lavana and end their fear.  He then asked his brothers who would undertake this task, suggesting either Bharata or Satrughna. Bharata volunteered, but Satrughna reminded Rama of Bharata's prior sacrifices, governing Ayodhya in Rama's absence, enduring hardship and patiently awaiting his return.  He described Bharata's ascetic life in Nandigrama, subsisting on fruits and roots while wearing matted hair and bark clothing.

summary:
Canto LXII
Rama enquires of the sages regarding Lavana’s way of living and dispatches
Satrughna with a view to kill Lavana
content: Rama decrees that Satrughna will be responsible for eliminating Lavana. He offers Satrughna the kingdom of Madhu, urging him to establish a new city near the Yamuna river and govern the surrounding prosperous provinces. Rama emphasizes the importance of appointing a ruler after conquering a territory, warning that failure to do so leads to undesirable consequences. He instructs Satrughna to kill Lavana and then rule the kingdom righteously, adhering to Rama's counsel.  Rama concludes by requesting Satrughna to accept the formal consecration ceremony, conducted by Vasistha and other Brahmins.

summary:
Canto LXIII

Rama coronates Satrughna as a king and explains to him the way to
safeguard himself against Lavana’s lance
content: After Rama's words, the mighty Satrughna, feeling ashamed, gently expressed his concerns about the propriety of being crowned before his elder brothers. He questioned how such an act could be considered righteous.  Satrughna insisted on obeying Rama's command, acknowledging the impropriety of speaking after Bharata's vow. He regretted his earlier boast of killing Lavana, attributing his current predicament to that statement.  Satrughna declared he would not speak out of turn again, asking Rama to spare him further reprimand. He pledged obedience to Rama, requesting that any wrongdoing resulting from his actions be forgiven.  Pleased with Satrughna's humility and obedience, Rama instructed Bharata and Lakshmana to gather the necessary items for the coronation. He commanded them to summon Vasistha, the chief priest, the townspeople, other priests, and the counselors. The great warriors, following Rama's orders, assembled the required materials, with Vasistha leading the preparations.  Kings and Brahmins entered the royal palace, and the consecration of Satrughna commenced.

summary:
Canto LXIII

Rama coronates Satrughna as a king and explains to him the way to
safeguard himself against Lavana’s lance
content: Rama, likened to the sun, crowned Satrughna, causing widespread joy. This coronation was reminiscent of Kartikeya's, bringing happiness to the citizens and Brahmins alike.  The royal mothers, including Kausalya, Sumitra, and Kaikeyi, performed auspicious rites within the palace.  The sages residing by the Yamuna River felt confident that Lavana's defeat was imminent due to Satrughna's coronation.  Following the ceremony, Rama embraced Satrughna and bestowed upon him a divine, unfailing arrow, declaring it the instrument of Lavana's demise. Rama explained the arrow's origin, stating it was created by Vishnu while he rested in the cosmic ocean, unseen by gods and demons.  This powerful, invisible arrow was the same one Vishnu used to destroy the evil Madhu and Kaitabha and create the three worlds. Rama revealed he refrained from using this arrow against Ravana to prevent widespread destruction. He then described Lavana's trident, a weapon gifted by Lord Shiva, which Lavana uses to procure food and incinerate any challenger. Rama instructed Satrughna to confront Lavana at the city's eastern gate before he could retrieve his trident, ensuring victory. By following this strategy, Satrughna would defeat Lavana, as Lord Shiva's will, though powerful, could be circumvented.

summary:
Canto LXIV
Satrughna dispatches his army in compliance with Sri Rama’s order in
advance and he himself proceedes after a month
content: Rama addressed Satrughna, outlining the logistics and strategy for his mission to Madhura.  He detailed provisions for Satrughna's journey: four thousand horses, two thousand chariots, one hundred elephants, and merchants with their goods.  Actors and dancers would also accompany him, along with a substantial sum of gold.  Rama emphasized the importance of maintaining the army's morale, urging Satrughna to keep his troops content and disciplined.  He highlighted the isolation of battle, where neither wealth, family, nor companions offer support, underscoring the vital role of a satisfied army.  Rama then laid out the strategic plan: Satrughna should first dispatch the army, ensuring their well-being and safe passage across the Ganga river before the monsoon season.  Only then should Satrughna himself proceed, discreetly and alone, to the forest of Madhu, where Lavana resided.  He cautioned Satrughna about Lavana's strength, emphasizing the need for a carefully timed attack.  The optimal time to strike, Rama advised, was at the end of summer, with the onset of the rainy season. Following Rama's instructions, Satrughna gathered the army's leaders and communicated the designated encampments, urging them to maintain peaceful coexistence during their journey.

summary:
Canto LXIV
Satrughna dispatches his army in compliance with Sri Rama’s order in
advance and he himself proceedes after a month
content: After dispatching the vast army, Satrughna paid his respects to Kausalya, Sumitra, and Kaikeyi.  He then respectfully circumambulated and bowed before Rama, and prostrated himself at the feet of Lakshmana, Bharata, and the chief priest Vasistha. With Rama's permission, the mighty Satrughna, known for his piety and as a formidable warrior, once again circumambulated Rama before departing.  He sent the army, replete with elephants and horses, ahead.  Satrughna remained with Rama for a month before finally embarking on his own journey.

summary:
Canto LXV

Sage Valmiki narrates to Satrughna the story of
Kalmasapada, son of Sudasa
content: Satrughna, after dispatching his army, quickly journeyed to Valmiki's hermitage.  He spent two nights traveling before arriving at the peaceful sanctuary.  Upon arrival, Satrughna respectfully greeted the sage and expressed his intention to stay briefly, explaining he had an important task to complete westward the following morning. Valmiki warmly welcomed Satrughna, reminding him the hermitage was open to all descendants of Raghu. He offered Satrughna refreshments and a place to rest.  Satrughna gratefully accepted the hospitality and, after enjoying the meal, inquired about the remnants of a sacrificial site he had noticed nearby.  Valmiki, acknowledging Satrughna's curiosity,  began to tell him the history of the site, explaining it had once belonged to a virtuous and courageous ancestor of Satrughna's named Virasaha, son of King Sudasa. Valmiki continued, recounting how the young but brave Sudasa encountered two insatiable Rakshasas during a hunting expedition.  These fearsome creatures, disguised as lions, had already devoured countless animals but remained hungry.

summary:
Canto LXV

Sage Valmiki narrates to Satrughna the story of
Kalmasapada, son of Sudasa
content: King Sudasa, enraged at the sight of two Rakshasas and a forest emptied of animals, killed one of them with an arrow.  After slaying the creature, Sudasa calmly observed the fallen Rakshasa.  The surviving Rakshasa, witnessing his companion's death, was filled with grief and vowed revenge on Sudasa for killing his innocent friend.  After making this threat, the Rakshasa vanished.  Later, when Sudasa, now known as Mitrasaha/Virasaha, became king, he held a grand Ashvamedha (horse sacrifice) near a hermitage.  Sage Vasistha participated in this elaborate ritual, which lasted for many years and rivaled even the sacrifices of the gods.  As the sacrifice concluded, the Rakshasa, disguised as Vasistha, reminded the king of his past deed and demanded a meal of flesh without delay. The king, believing he was addressing Vasistha, instructed the cooks to prepare a meat dish fit for the sage.  The head cook was disturbed by this request, and the Rakshasa, seizing the opportunity, disguised himself as the cook and presented the king with human flesh for the meal.

summary:
Canto LXV

Sage Valmiki narrates to Satrughna the story of
Kalmasapada, son of Sudasa
content: King Virasaha and his wife Madayanti unknowingly offered human flesh, brought by a Rakshasa disguised as a cook, to Sage Vasistha.  Vasistha, realizing the nature of the meal, became enraged and cursed the king.  Although the queen tried to intervene, reminding Virasaha of Vasistha's revered position, the king, still under the influence of the Rakshasa's deception, proceeded with a symbolic curse, sprinkling water on his own feet. This act resulted in his feet becoming discolored, earning him the name Kalmasapada.  Upon realizing the truth, the king and queen pleaded with Vasistha, who, though unable to fully retract the curse, lessened its duration to twelve years and ensured the king would not remember the incident.  Kalmasapada then returned to his duties, ruling his kingdom.  Sage Valmiki concluded this part of the story by identifying the location of Kalmasapada's sacrifice, which Satrughna had inquired about.  After hearing this tale, Satrughna respectfully entered his dwelling.

summary:
Canto LXVI

Birth of two sons to Sita, Valmiki makes arrangement for their protection.
Satrughna is happy over this and leaves for the bank of the Yamuna
content: Satrughna arrived at the hermitage where Sita resided. That same night, Sita gave birth to twin sons. At midnight, young ascetics informed Sage Valmiki of the joyous news. They requested him to perform protective rites for the newborns to ward off evil spirits.  Valmiki, delighted, went to see the infants, radiant as the moon. He performed the sacred rituals for their protection.  Using kusa grass, he created a protective amulet for each child. The firstborn was named Kusha, and the younger twin, Lava.  Valmiki declared that these names would bring them renown.  The women of the hermitage reverently received the amulets from Valmiki and performed the protective ceremonies.  Meanwhile, Satrughna heard the celebratory chanting of Rama's lineage and the news of the births.  He entered Sita's dwelling and expressed his joy at the arrival of her two sons. The night passed quickly for Satrughna, filled with happiness at this auspicious event.

summary:
Canto LXVI

Birth of two sons to Sita, Valmiki makes arrangement for their protection.
Satrughna is happy over this and leaves for the bank of the Yamuna
content: Satrughna, after performing his morning rituals, respectfully bid farewell to Sage Valmiki and journeyed westward.  His travels took him seven nights to reach the banks of the Yamuna river.  There, he sought shelter and companionship in the hermitages of revered sages, including the prominent Sage Bhargava.  Satrughna, a renowned figure himself, engaged in meaningful discussions with these holy men.  He spent the night in their company, particularly with Sage Cyavana, listening to and sharing various enlightening and spiritual stories.

Satrughna inquired about Lavana's strength and his trident's power, asking who had fallen to it in battle. Sage Cyavana recounted Lavana's history, focusing on an incident involving King Mandhata, a powerful ruler who sought to conquer the heavens.  Mandhata, having established his rule over Earth, set his sights on the celestial realm, alarming the gods.  Despite Indra's warning that he hadn't fully subdued Earth, Mandhata challenged the notion, claiming complete control. Indra revealed that the Rakshasa Lavana, son of Madhu, defied Mandhata's authority, preventing his total dominion over the earth.

King Mandhata, humiliated by Indra's revelation of Lavana's defiance, returned to earth to subdue the Rakshasa.  He dispatched a messenger to Lavana, who, upon receiving the message, killed the messenger.  Enraged by this, Mandhata attacked Lavana with arrows.  Lavana retaliated with his powerful trident, incinerating Mandhata, his army, and his entourage.  The sage Cyavana concluded his story by assuring Satrughna that Lavana's trident was indeed immensely powerful but vulnerable when Lavana was hunting. He encouraged Satrughna, stating that victory was certain if he struck Lavana in the morning while he was unarmed and hunting, and that this act would bring peace to the world.

summary:
Canto LXVIII
Lavana goes out in search of food. Satrughna stays at the citygate
awaiting return of Lavana and has an angry exchange
of words with the latter
content: While others at the hermitage wished Satrughna victory, the night quickly passed.  At daybreak, Lavana, driven by hunger, left the city. Meanwhile, Satrughna, armed with his bow, stood at Madhu's gate.  At midday, Lavana returned, carrying his gruesome burden of slain creatures.  Seeing Satrughna, Lavana taunted him, boasting of his past victims and mocking Satrughna's arrival as a willing meal.  Enraged by Lavana's words, Satrughna's body blazed with fury.  He declared his identity as Satrughna, brother of Rama, and challenged Lavana to a duel, vowing to prevent his escape.

summary:
Canto LXVIII
Lavana goes out in search of food. Satrughna stays at the citygate
awaiting return of Lavana and has an angry exchange
of words with the latter
content: Lavana mocked Satrughna, attributing his arrival to good luck. He taunted Satrughna about Rama's victory over Ravana, claiming to have forgiven the destruction of Ravana's family, but now intending to destroy Rama's lineage.  He boasted that he would sweep away Satrughna and all his kin like straws.  Lavana then offered Satrughna a duel, saying he would fetch his weapon first.  Satrughna retorted that Lavana would not escape alive, declaring that a disciplined warrior should not allow a willing enemy to flee. He vowed to send Lavana to the afterlife with his arrows, calling him an enemy of the three worlds and the Raghu dynasty.

summary:
Canto LXIX

Duel fight between Satrughna and Lavana,
Satrughna kills Lavana
content: Satrughna and Lavana exchanged heated words, provoking Lavana into a furious rage.  He challenged Satrughna, rubbing his hands together and gnashing his teeth. Satrughna responded defiantly, prophesying Lavana's imminent death and the peace that would follow. He declared his arrow would pierce Lavana's heart as surely as sunlight penetrates a lotus. Enraged, Lavana hurled a massive tree at Satrughna, who swiftly reduced it to splinters with his arrows.  Undeterred, Lavana continued his assault with more trees, which Satrughna effortlessly destroyed. Satrughna then unleashed a barrage of arrows upon Lavana, but the demon remained unharmed.  Finally, Lavana struck Satrughna on the head with a tree, causing him to lose consciousness.

summary:
Canto LXIX

Duel fight between Satrughna and Lavana,
Satrughna kills Lavana
content: Upon Satrughna's fall, the sages, gods, Gandharvas, and Apsaras cried out in alarm.  Lavana, mistaking Satrughna's unconsciousness for death, left him without entering his dwelling.  He even retrieved his supplies, believing his foe to be defeated.  However, Satrughna quickly regained consciousness, and armed himself, standing at Madhu's gate, revered by the sages. He then grasped a divine, radiant arrow, its brilliance illuminating the world. This arrow, resembling a thunderbolt, was said to be invincible and caused great fear among all who saw it.  The gods, concerned by the arrow's power, approached Brahma, inquiring if the end of the world was near. Brahma reassured them, explaining it was Vishnu's ancient arrow, intended for Madhu and Kaitabha, and now wielded by Satrughna to slay Lavana.  He urged the gods to witness the battle.  Upon hearing this, they gathered in the sky above the battlefield. Seeing them, and the potent arrow in Satrughna’s hand, Lavana was once again challenged by a roaring Satrughna.

summary:
Canto LXIX

Duel fight between Satrughna and Lavana,
Satrughna kills Lavana
content: Satrughna, wielding a divine arrow, faced Lavana, who was enraged and ready for battle.  Satrughna shot the arrow, which pierced Lavana's chest and descended to the netherworld before returning to him.  Struck by the powerful arrow, Lavana fell lifelessly to the ground.  The divine arrow then returned to Rudra's quiver as the gods watched. With this single shot, Satrughna, the champion of the Raghus, vanquished the terror of the three worlds.  Gods, sages, serpents, and Apsaras celebrated Satrughna's fearless victory and the end of Lavana's reign of terror.

The Raksasa horde, roaring like a storm, unleashed a barrage of missiles upon Narayana, who stood firm as a mountain.  Vishnu, radiant despite his dark complexion, was surrounded by the shadowy figures of the Raksasas, like a hill of antimony amidst dark clouds.  The Raksasas' arrows, swift as lightning, thought, and wind itself, pierced Vishnu as locusts swarm a field, moths a flame, bees a honey pot, crocodiles the sea, and as the worlds return to Vishnu at the time of cosmic dissolution.  Towering like mountains, the Raksasa chiefs, some in chariots, others atop elephants and horses, and still others fighting on foot in the air, assailed Vishnu with a relentless storm of arrows, javelins, swords, and iron clubs.  Like an ocean besieged by fish, Vishnu, the indomitable, returned fire, drawing his bow Sarnga and loosing arrows at his attackers. With arrows sharp and hard as diamonds, Vishnu decimated the Raksasas by the hundreds and thousands.  Having scattered his foes like wind dispersing rain clouds, the supreme being blew his mighty conch Panchajanya. The resounding blast echoed through the three worlds, filling them with a terrifying sound.  Like a lion's roar in the jungle scattering panicked elephants, the conch's sound unnerved the Raksasas.  Horses lost their footing, elephants their rage, and soldiers tumbled from their chariots.  The finely feathered arrows launched from the Sarnga bow tore through the Raksasas, their diamond-hard tips burying themselves in the earth.  Pierced by Narayana's arrows, the Raksasas collapsed like mountains struck by thunderbolts.

Vishnu's attack caused significant bloodshed among the Raksasas.  The sounds of Vishnu's conch and bow overpowered the Raksasas' cries.  Vishnu's arrows destroyed their weapons, chariots, and banners. Like a powerful force of nature, Vishnu's arrows flew swiftly and abundantly, decimating the Raksasa army.  The Raksasas fled in terror before Vishnu's might, much like a chain of animals fleeing from a predator.  After slaying thousands, Vishnu blew his conch. The remaining Raksasas, terrified by Vishnu's arrows and conch, retreated towards Lanka.  Sumali, a Raksasa leader, attempted to stop Vishnu's advance with a barrage of arrows, briefly rallying his comrades.  Another Raksasa, emboldened, roared and charged towards Vishnu, but Vishnu swiftly retaliated by killing his charioteer, causing the Raksasa's horses to panic and flee.

Vishnu's relentless assault continued.  Sumali, the Raksasa lord, found his chariot thrown into chaos by his panicked horses, while another Raksasa, Mali, bravely confronted Vishnu.  Despite Mali's arrows finding their mark, Vishnu remained unfazed.  Vishnu retaliated, his arrows drawing blood from Mali as eagerly as serpents drink nectar.  Mali's chariot, weapons, and even his crest were cast down by Vishnu's power.  Seizing his club, Mali struck Garuda, Vishnu's mount, causing Vishnu to momentarily turn from the battle. The Raksasas roared in triumph, but their celebration was short-lived. Enraged, Vishnu hurled his discus, severing Mali's head, which fell to the earth like a gruesome meteor.  The gods rejoiced at this victory.  Sumali and Malyavan, grief-stricken by Mali's death, retreated with their remaining forces.  Garuda, recovered from the blow, scattered the fleeing Raksasas with powerful gusts from his wings.  The Raksasas fell from the sky, their bodies broken and mangled by Vishnu's varied weapons. Vishnu continued his attack, his arrows tearing through the Raksasa ranks like lightning bolts through a mountain.  The remaining Raksasas, their armor shattered, weapons lost, and bodies wounded, were completely routed.  Like elephants fleeing a lion, they cried out in terror and desperately tried to escape, their own arrows proving futile against Vishnu's relentless barrage.

Vishnu's devastating attack continued.  Raksasa leaders fell like mountains struck by lightning, their heads severed by his discus, limbs crushed by his club, and bodies bisected by his sword. As the slain Raksasas, adorned with gold necklaces and earrings, plummeted to the earth, they resembled dark clouds or blue mountains crashing down, covering the ground with their fallen forms.

summary:
Canto LXX

Having obtained a boon from the gods and after founding Madhupurt,
in the twelfth year, Satrughna decides to meet Sri Rama
content: After Lavana's defeat, the gods, led by Indra and Agni, congratulated Satrughna. They offered him a boon for his victory.  Satrughna requested that the city of Madhu, built by the gods, be established as his capital, Madhura. The gods granted his wish, proclaiming it would become a prosperous city named Shurasena. Satrughna then led his army back from the Ganga.  Upon hearing of Lavana's demise, the army quickly returned and began establishing the capital in the month of Sravana. Over twelve years, the city flourished under Satrughna's rule, becoming a safe and prosperous land. The fields were fertile, with timely rains, and the people were healthy. Madhura, shaped like a half-moon along the Yamuna river, was adorned with grand houses, bustling markets, and a diverse population engaged in various trades. Satrughna further enhanced the city, adding colorful decorations to Lavana's former mansion, constructing rest houses and gardens, and adorning it with beautiful features, both human-made and natural.

summary:
Canto LXX

Having obtained a boon from the gods and after founding Madhupurt,
in the twelfth year, Satrughna decides to meet Sri Rama
content: In the twelfth year of his reign, Satrughna, Bharata's younger brother, surveyed the flourishing city of Madhura with immense satisfaction.  The city, divinely radiant and bustling with diverse merchandise, attracted traders from various lands.  Witnessing this prosperity, Satrughna decided to journey to see Lord Rama.  Having established and populated the magnificent city, reminiscent of a celestial abode, he resolved to seek the blessings of his elder brother.

summary:
Canto LXXI
Satrughna, accompanied by a few soldiers, proceeds to Ayodhya, on the
way listening Sri Rama’s glory being sung at Rsi Valmiki’s Asrama
and getting pleasantly surprised
content: Satrughna, accompanied by a small contingent of soldiers and attendants, set out for Ayodhya, now under Rama's rule, twelve years after Rama's return.  He dismissed the main army and chiefs, choosing to travel with a select group and a hundred chariots, riding a special horse.  After several stops along the way, he arrived at the hermitage of Sage Valmiki and stayed there.  Satrughna respectfully greeted the sage and accepted his hospitality.  Valmiki entertained Satrughna with numerous captivating stories. The sage recounted Satrughna's victory over Lavana, praising him for this difficult feat. He explained how Lavana had previously defeated many powerful kings and their armies. Valmiki emphasized that Satrughna had effortlessly accomplished this challenging task, while Rama's victory over Ravana had required great effort. The sage declared that the gods and all beings rejoiced at Lavana's defeat, a truly commendable deed.  Valmiki further revealed that he had witnessed the battle with Lavana while in Indra's assembly. Overwhelmed with joy, Valmiki expressed his affection for Satrughna, desiring to smell his head as a gesture of profound love and respect.

summary:
Canto LXXI
Satrughna, accompanied by a few soldiers, proceeds to Ayodhya, on the
way listening Sri Rama’s glory being sung at Rsi Valmiki’s Asrama
and getting pleasantly surprised
content: After smelling Satrughna's head as a gesture of respect, the wise Valmiki offered hospitality to him and his followers. Satrughna partook of the meal and then listened to a melodious recitation of Rama's deeds. The performance was accompanied by a lute, drums, and vocals, all in Sanskrit and adhering to traditional musical rules. The song recounted Rama's story accurately, as if the events were unfolding in the present moment. Satrughna was deeply moved, tears welling in his eyes, and he even briefly lost consciousness, sighing deeply. His accompanying warriors were also awestruck by the beautiful song, speechless with wonder. They questioned amongst themselves and Satrughna whether they were dreaming, having seen and heard such marvels in the hermitage. Satrughna, however, cautioned them against questioning the sage out of mere curiosity, acknowledging the many wonders of the hermitage but advising respectful restraint. After saluting Valmiki, Satrughna retired to his quarters.

summary:
Canto LXXII

After taking leave from Valmiki, Satrughna meets Sri Rama
and others at Ayodhya and after staying there
for a week, leaves again for Madhupurt
content: Satrughna, deeply contemplating the epic tale of Rama's life, found sleep elusive.  The melodious strains of a lute playing the Ramayana filled the night, allowing it to pass quickly for him.  After completing his morning rituals, Satrughna respectfully addressed the sage Valmiki, expressing his desire to visit Rama in Ayodhya and seeking permission to depart. Valmiki embraced Satrughna and granted his request.  Satrughna then journeyed to Ayodhya, eager to see Rama.  Upon arriving, he entered the palace and found Rama amidst his advisors, radiant as the full moon amongst the stars. Satrughna respectfully greeted Rama and informed him of the successful completion of his mission: Lavana had been defeated, and Madhupuri was restored.  He then expressed his deep longing to remain by Rama's side, stating that twelve years of separation was enough and that he could no longer bear to live apart, like a calf separated from its mother. Rama embraced Satrughna and consoled him, urging him not to despair and reminding him of the expected fortitude of a Kshatriya warrior.

summary:
Canto LXXII

After taking leave from Valmiki, Satrughna meets Sri Rama
and others at Ayodhya and after staying there
for a week, leaves again for Madhupurt
content: Rama advised Satrughna that kings should not be disheartened by time spent away from their kingdoms, as their duty is to protect their people.  He encouraged Satrughna to visit Ayodhya from time to time, but emphasized the importance of Satrughna's presence in Madhura, assuring him of his deep affection.  Rama requested Satrughna to stay for seven nights before returning to Madhura with his entourage.  Satrughna accepted Rama's words with a heavy heart. After the allotted time, he bid farewell to Rama, Bharata, and Lakshmana, and departed in his chariot.  Lakshmana and Bharata accompanied him on foot for a considerable distance before he continued to Madhupuri.

summary:
Canto LXXIII
A Brahmana arrives at the palace-gate, carrying the dead

body of his son, and laments blaming the king
for the death of the child
content: An elderly Brahmin arrived at the palace gates carrying the body of his young son.  Overcome with grief, he repeatedly cried out, lamenting the loss of his only child.  He questioned what past deed could have merited such a tragedy, as his son had not even reached adolescence, dying prematurely before completing fourteen years of life. The Brahmin predicted his own imminent death from sorrow, along with his wife's.  He asserted his innocence, claiming never to have lied or sinned against any creature. He wondered what wrongdoing could have caused his son to die so young, before performing his father's last rites.  The Brahmin had never witnessed such an untimely death in Rama's kingdom. He attributed the child's death to a sin committed by Rama, claiming children in other kingdoms faced no such threat.  He demanded Rama restore his son to life, threatening to die with his wife at the palace gates, accusing Rama of the sin of brahmahatya (killing a Brahmin) if he did not comply.  Despite this accusation, the Brahmin acknowledged the prior peace and prosperity enjoyed under Rama's reign.

summary:
Canto LXXIII
A Brahmana arrives at the palace-gate, carrying the dead

body of his son, and laments blaming the king
for the death of the child
content: The Brahmin continued his lament, claiming that since his son's death occurred during Rama's reign, it must be due to Rama's governance.  He stated that being under Rama's rule now meant being under the sway of death, with little hope for happiness.  He declared the Ikshvaku dynasty's reign, under Rama, to be an unfortunate one where children die. He asserted that subjects perish due to the faults of their kings, particularly when the king is unjust, leading to untimely deaths. He reasoned that if improprieties occur in the kingdom without proper protection or justice, then such untimely deaths are a consequence. He concluded that his son's death must be due to some fault of Rama's within the city or provinces.  Overwhelmed by grief, the Brahmin repeatedly embraced his son's body, continuing these accusations against Rama.

summary:
Canto LXXIV

Narada told Sri Rama the cause of the death of Brahmana’s son
as an unauthorized practice of penance by a Siidra
content: A grieving Brahmana approached King Rama, lamenting a great sorrow.  Rama, deeply concerned, summoned his advisors, including Vasistha and Vamadeva, along with his brothers and other officials.  Eight esteemed Brahmanas, including Markandeya, Maudgalya, Vamadeva, Kasyapa, Katyayana, Jabali, Gautama, and Narada, were then brought before the king. After they were seated, Rama respectfully inquired about the Brahmana's distress. Narada explained the untimely death of the Brahmana’s son, urging Rama to address the situation.  He then described the decline of Dharma across the ages. In the Krta Yuga, only Brahmanas performed austerities. Everyone was immortal and clairvoyant. The Treta Yuga saw the rise of Kshatriyas, who also began to practice austerities, although not as effectively as the Brahmanas of the previous age.  In Treta Yuga, the four Varnas (castes) were established. However, Adharma (unrighteousness) began to creep in, symbolized by its "foot" touching the earth.  This was manifested in the rise of activities considered less pure, like agriculture.  The spread of falsehood further shortened lifespans compared to the Satya Yuga. Despite this, many people continued to perform virtuous deeds, adhering to truth and righteousness. During the Treta Yuga, Brahmanas and Kshatriyas continued their austerities, while Vaishyas and Shudras focused on service and supported the other three castes.

summary:
Canto LXXIV

Narada told Sri Rama the cause of the death of Brahmana’s son
as an unauthorized practice of penance by a Siidra
content: Narada continued his explanation to Rama about the decline of Dharma.  He described how in the Treta Yuga,  improper conduct and falsehood increased, causing further deterioration for Brahmanas and Kshatriyas. Unrighteousness gained a stronger foothold, ushering in the Dvapara Yuga. During the Dvapara Yuga, the practice of austerities extended to the Vaishyas.  Narada emphasized that across these three Yugas (Krita, Treta, and Dvapara), Dharma was upheld by the first three castes, with Shudras traditionally not engaging in such practices.  However, he warned that in the future Kali Yuga, even Shudras would undertake austerities, citing the current instance of a Shudra performing unauthorized penance in Rama's kingdom as a grave transgression against Dharma. This, Narada asserted, was the cause of the Brahmana's son's death.  He stressed that such actions depleted the kingdom's prosperity and would lead the king to ruin.  Narada urged Rama to investigate and rectify this unrighteous act within his kingdom, promising that doing so would restore Dharma, increase lifespans, and even bring the child back to life.

summary:
Canto LXXV
Sri Rama goes round in the aerial celestial car Puspaka of Kubera, in
order to trace out unrighteousness in his kingdom, but finding
no trace of it anywhere, He proceeds to the south and
comes across a Sidra practising penance
content: Rama, deeply moved by Narada's words, instructed Lakshmana to console the grieving Brahmana and preserve the deceased child's body in an oil tub filled with fragrant oils to prevent decomposition.  Following this, Rama mentally summoned the celestial chariot Puspaka. The golden, ornate Puspaka arrived promptly, offering its service to Rama. After respectfully greeting the sages, Rama boarded the chariot, armed with his bow, quiver, and sword. Leaving Lakshmana and Bharata in the city, Rama flew westward over the verdant land, then north towards the Himalayas. Finding no trace of unrighteousness, he surveyed the pristine eastern region.  Finally, he turned south, where virtuous conduct prevailed. There, beside Mount Saivala, he discovered a large lake and observed an ascetic engaged in intense penance, suspended upside down within its waters.

summary:
Canto LXXV
Sri Rama goes round in the aerial celestial car Puspaka of Kubera, in
order to trace out unrighteousness in his kingdom, but finding
no trace of it anywhere, He proceeds to the south and
comes across a Sidra practising penance
content: Rama approached the ascetic, who was engaged in rigorous penance, and commended him for his devotion. Driven by curiosity, Rama introduced himself as the son of Dasaratha and inquired about the ascetic's caste and the purpose of his arduous austerities.  He questioned whether the ascetic sought heaven or some other boon through these difficult practices. Rama further asked the ascetic to reveal the specific aim of his penance and to truthfully declare his caste, whether Brahmana, Kshatriya, Vaishya, or Shudra.  Upon being questioned by Rama, the ascetic prepared to disclose his caste and the motivation behind his intense spiritual endeavors.

summary:
Canto LXXVI

Sti Rama kills Sambika for which gods laud Him. Sage Agastya
welcomes Sri Rama and presents a celestial ornament to Him
content: A hermit, hanging his head low, addressed Rama, revealing his low-caste birth and his desire to achieve godhood through penance.  He identified himself as the Shudra, Shambuka.  Rama, drawing his shining sword, swiftly beheaded Shambuka.  The gods, led by Indra and Agni, rejoiced, showering praise upon Rama. Fragrant celestial flowers rained down from the heavens. The pleased deities commended Rama's valorous act, offering him any boon he desired.  Rama, however, requested that the Brahmin's son, who had died prematurely due to some unwitting fault of Rama's, be restored to life. He reminded the gods of his promise to the grieving father and expressed his desire to uphold his word.

summary:
Canto LXXVI

Sti Rama kills Sambika for which gods laud Him. Sage Agastya
welcomes Sri Rama and presents a celestial ornament to Him
content: Upon Rama's request to revive the Brahmin's son, the gods rejoiced and informed Rama that the boy had been restored to life the moment Shambuka was slain.  They blessed Rama and then announced their intention to visit Sage Agastya, whose twelve-year vow involving sleeping on water had just concluded.  The gods invited Rama to join them.  Rama agreed and boarded his chariot, Puspaka.  The gods departed on their celestial vehicles, with Rama following to Agastya's hermitage. Upon their arrival, Agastya respectfully welcomed and honored the gods.  After the gods departed for heaven, Rama descended from his chariot and paid his respects to the radiant Sage Agastya, accepting the sage's hospitality and taking a seat.

summary:
Canto LXXVI

Sti Rama kills Sambika for which gods laud Him. Sage Agastya
welcomes Sri Rama and presents a celestial ornament to Him
content: Sage Agastya welcomed Rama, praising him as the best among men and expressing his good fortune at Rama's arrival.  He honored Rama as a cherished guest, whose virtues made him worthy of adoration. Agastya acknowledged Rama's recent actions, stating that the gods had informed him of Shambuka's death and the subsequent revival of the Brahmin's son. He urged Rama to stay the night, recognizing him as the divine Narayana, in whom all things reside, and the lord of all gods, the eternal Purusha.  Agastya then offered Rama a divinely crafted ornament made by Visvakarma, encouraging him to accept it as a gift that would bring further merit upon being given again.  He emphasized Rama's worthiness to possess such an ornament, highlighting his capacity for generosity and his power to protect even the gods.  Rama, mindful of his Kshatriya duties, questioned the propriety of accepting a gift, particularly from a Brahmin, citing the prohibition against Kshatriyas receiving donations. He requested clarification from the sage.  Agastya responded by recounting a tale from the Satyayuga, the age of Brahma, when the world lacked a king.  The people appealed to Brahma for a ruler, so they could worship him and live free of sin. Brahma summoned the guardians of the regions and Indra, requesting a portion of their power.  They complied, and Brahma, upon sneezing, created King Ksupa, whom he invested with the combined might of the guardians.

summary:
Canto LXXVI

Sti Rama kills Sambika for which gods laud Him. Sage Agastya
welcomes Sri Rama and presents a celestial ornament to Him
content: Agastya explained that Ksupa, the first king, ruled by the combined power bestowed upon him by various gods. Ksupa nurtured the people with Varuna's power, shared Kubera's prosperity, and upheld justice with Yama's authority.  Agastya then offered Rama a dazzling, sun-like ornament, requesting Rama accept it for Agastya’s own spiritual benefit.  Rama, accepting the gift, respectfully inquired about its origins, expressing his curiosity about its acquisition, recognizing Agastya's profound wisdom.  Agastya, in response, began a tale from the Treta Yuga.

summary:
Canto LXXVII

Sage Agastya narrates the story of the king Sveta,
who after eating corpse acquires heaven
content: In the ancient Tretayuga, a vast, desolate forest stretched for a hundred yojanas, devoid of animals and birds.  Sage Agastya, seeking solitude for his austerities, entered this uninhabited forest. He found it a place of unexpected beauty, filled with fruit-bearing trees and a vast lake teeming with swans, ducks, and Cakrawaka birds. The lake, covered in lotuses and lilies, was a source of great wonder and tranquility.  Nearby, he discovered a magnificent, ancient hermitage, though unoccupied. Agastya spent a summer night there.  The next morning, he went to the lake and discovered a pristine, unblemished corpse floating in the water.  As he pondered this strange sight, a radiant, swan-drawn chariot descended from the sky. A celestial being, adorned with heavenly ornaments and attended by nymphs who sang, played music, and danced, emerged.  Descending from the chariot, the celestial being consumed the corpse while Agastya watched in astonishment.  Afterward, the being performed ablutions in the lake before preparing to depart.  Agastya, overcome with curiosity, questioned the celestial being's identity and the reason for consuming the corpse. The celestial being, moved by Agastya's respectful inquiry, agreed to explain.

summary:
Canto LXXVIII
King Sveta tells Agastya the reason as to why he had to eat the
condemned food and recounts his conversation with
Brahma, He gives Agastya a divine ornament
and gets freed from hunger and thirst
content: King Sveta narrates his story to Agastya.  He explains that after hearing Rama's divinely inspired words, he felt compelled to share his past. Sveta recounts how, as the elder son of the renowned King Sudeva, he inherited the kingdom and ruled righteously for a thousand years.  Forewarned of his approaching death, Sveta appointed his younger brother, Suratha, as king and retreated to a remote forest to perform penances. After three thousand years of arduous austerities, he ascended to Brahma's celestial realm.  However, upon reaching this supposedly perfect world, Sveta was unexpectedly plagued by intense hunger and thirst.  Confused and distressed, he approached Brahma to inquire about the cause of his suffering and what he should eat.

summary:
Canto LXXVIII
King Sveta tells Agastya the reason as to why he had to eat the
condemned food and recounts his conversation with
Brahma, He gives Agastya a divine ornament
and gets freed from hunger and thirst
content: Brahma decreed that King Sveta's own flesh would be his sustenance, explaining that since Sveta had not performed acts of charity, he was now afflicted by hunger and thirst in the celestial realm. Brahma assured Sveta that his body, nourished by years of austerities, would regenerate, serving as a perpetual food source.  Brahma further prophesied that Sage Agastya would eventually arrive and release Sveta from this predicament.  Upon hearing this, Sveta began consuming his own flesh, which miraculously did not diminish over time. He then implored Agastya to free him from this cycle.  As an offering, Sveta presented Agastya with a divine ornament, gold, wealth, clothing, food, and other valuable possessions, beseeching the sage's favor.

summary:
Canto LXXVIII
King Sveta tells Agastya the reason as to why he had to eat the
condemned food and recounts his conversation with
Brahma, He gives Agastya a divine ornament
and gets freed from hunger and thirst
content: King Sveta, deeply saddened by Brahma's decree, offered Agastya a magnificent ornament in hopes of relief. Upon accepting this gift, Sveta's afflicted body vanished.  Overjoyed, the king ascended to the heavens.  Agastya then explained to Rama that Sveta, a king comparable to Indra, had bestowed this wondrous ornament upon him as an offering in exchange for release from his suffering.

summary:
Canto LXXIX
Reign of king Danda, a son of Iksvaku
content: Sage Agastya recounted a tale from the Krtayuga to Rama.  Manu, the then king, appointed his son Iksvaku as his successor, instructing him to establish a lineage of kings and rule justly. Manu emphasized the importance of appropriate punishment for the guilty, which would lead a righteous king to heaven.  After giving this counsel, Manu ascended to Brahma's abode through deep meditation. Iksvaku, now king, contemplated how he would fulfill his father's wish to continue the royal line.

summary:
Canto LXXIX
Reign of king Danda, a son of Iksvaku
content: King Iksvaku, son of Manu, fathered one hundred sons.  His youngest son, however, was unlearned and disrespectful towards his elders.  Iksvaku named this son Danda, believing he was destined for punishment.  Lacking a suitably harsh territory for his wayward son, Iksvaku granted Danda the land between the Vindhya and Saivala mountains.  There, Danda established a magnificent city, naming it Madhumanta, and appointed the devout Usana as his chief priest. King Danda, with Usana's counsel, ruled this kingdom prosperously, much like Indra ruling over the celestial realm.

summary:
Canto VIII

Malyavan’s fight and his defeat; retreat of Sumalt and other
Raksasas into the nether world
content: Malyavan returned to the battlefield, enraged by the slaughter of his forces.  He challenged Padmanabha (Vishnu), accusing him of violating the rules of war by attacking those who had ceased fighting.  Malyavan boasted of his own strength and urged Vishnu to demonstrate his prowess.  Vishnu replied that he was fulfilling a promise to protect the gods from the Rakshasas, and would destroy them even if they fled to the underworld.  As Vishnu spoke, Malyavan hurled a javelin, which struck Vishnu's chest. Vishnu then pulled out the javelin and hurled it back at Malyavan with great force.

summary:
Canto VIII

Malyavan’s fight and his defeat; retreat of Sumalt and other
Raksasas into the nether world
content: Malyavan, struck by Vishnu's weapon, momentarily faltered but quickly recovered. He then attacked Vishnu and Garuda with a spiked iron weapon.  Garuda retaliated by creating a powerful gust of wind with his wings, driving Malyavan back. Witnessing this, Sumali and Malyavan, along with their remaining forces, retreated to Lanka.  Many Rakshasas, defeated by Vishnu and having lost their leaders, sought refuge in the netherworld with their wives, under the protection of Sumali, a descendant of Salakatankata.  These Rakshasas, including Sumali, Malyavan, and Mali, were exceptionally powerful, even more so than Ravana.

summary:
Canto VIII

Malyavan’s fight and his defeat; retreat of Sumalt and other
Raksasas into the nether world
content: The narrator describes the unparalleled power of Lord Narayana, the only one capable of vanquishing the Rakshasas, who were a constant threat to the gods. He is praised as the eternal, four-armed Lord, born to eradicate these demonic beings.  The narrator emphasizes Narayana's role as the protector of those who seek refuge in him, appearing in times of need to restore order and dharma by eliminating those who disregard it.  Addressing the king, the narrator confirms having explained the origin of the Rakshasas and offers to continue the story by detailing the birth, life, and immense power of Ravana and his son.  The narrative then shifts to Sumali, a powerful Rakshasa, who, fearing Vishnu, wandered the netherworld with his offspring.  During this time, Kubera (Vaisravana), the god of wealth, established himself in Lanka.

summary:
Canto LXXX

The story of Dandakavana. King Danda violates the chastity
of Bhargava’s daughter and is cursed by him
content: King Danda, after ruling peacefully for ten thousand years, visited the hermitage of Sage Bhargava. There, he encountered Bhargava's daughter, Araja, and, overcome by desire, attempted to seduce her. Araja firmly rejected his advances, reminding him of his student-teacher relationship with her father and warning him of the dire consequences that would follow if he persisted. She advised him to seek her hand in marriage through her father, the proper and respectful way, rather than resorting to force.  She cautioned that her father's wrath, if provoked, could be devastating. Despite her clear warning, Danda, blinded by lust, continued to pursue her.

summary:
Canto LXXX

The story of Dandakavana. King Danda violates the chastity
of Bhargava’s daughter and is cursed by him
content: Danda, despite Araja's pleas and warnings, forcibly took her.  He then returned to his city, Madhumanta. Araja, deeply distressed, remained near the hermitage, awaiting her father's return.

summary:
Canto LXXXI
End of king Danda, his family and kingdom,

as a result of
content: Bhargava, the divine Rishi, returned to his hermitage, hungry but enraged by what he saw.  He found Araja covered in semen, her beauty diminished like a moon eclipsed at dawn.  His anger, intensified by hunger, burned within him. He warned his pupils that Danda, ignorant and acting against righteousness, would face severe consequences for his actions, like touching a flame.  Danda's sin would bring its own punishment. Within seven nights, the king, his family, army, and all he possessed would be destroyed.  Indra would rain down dust, obliterating Danda's kingdom for a hundred leagues in every direction. All life within that area, both moving and stationary, would perish. After seven nights, Danda's once-prosperous kingdom would vanish entirely.  Bhargava, eyes blazing with fury, instructed his followers to leave the area and seek refuge elsewhere.  Heeding his words, the inhabitants of the hermitage departed Danda's lands.

summary:
Canto LXXXI
End of king Danda, his family and kingdom,

as a result of
content: Bhargava instructed the remorseful Araja to remain at the hermitage and assured her that any beings seeking refuge there that night would be protected from the impending disaster. Araja, though saddened, accepted her father's decree. Bhargava then departed.  Within seven days, as foretold, King Danda's kingdom, along with his family, army, and possessions, was reduced to ashes. The area, situated between the Vindhya and Saivala mountains, became known as Dandakaranya because of Danda's unrighteous actions. Later, due to the presence of ascetics, it was called Janasthana.  The narrator concludes by urging Rama to perform his evening rituals as the sun has set and the sages have completed their prayers.

summary:
Canto LXXXII
Sri Rama leaves Agastya’s hermitage and starts for Ayodhya
content: Rama, having listened respectfully to Agastya, went to a sacred lake frequented by Apsaras for his evening prayers. After praying, he returned to Agastya's hermitage.  Agastya offered Rama wholesome food including fine radishes and rice. Rama enjoyed the meal and spent the night at the hermitage.  The next morning, after performing his morning rituals, Rama approached Agastya to request permission to leave for Ayodhya.  Rama expressed his gratitude for the sage's hospitality and promised to visit again. Agastya was pleased by Rama's respectful words and affirmed Rama's purifying presence, stating that even a glimpse of Rama cleanses a person and grants them access to heaven, earning them the reverence of the gods. Conversely, those who look upon Rama with hostility will face dire consequences. Agastya concluded by extolling Rama's virtuous nature and declaring that recounting Rama's deeds on earth leads to spiritual fulfillment.

summary:
Canto LXXXII
Sri Rama leaves Agastya’s hermitage and starts for Ayodhya
content: Agastya blessed Rama, advising him to rule his kingdom righteously and peacefully.  Rama respectfully saluted the sage and the other ascetics before boarding the golden Puspaka.  The sages showered Rama with blessings as he ascended, comparing him to Indra, the king of the gods.  Rama, seated on the Puspaka, shone like the moon emerging from behind the clouds. By midday, having been honored along the way, Rama arrived in Ayodhya and landed in the center of the city. He then dismissed the Puspaka, wishing it well on its return to Kubera.  Finally, Rama instructed a doorkeeper to swiftly summon Lakshmana and Bharata, informing them of his return.

summary:
Canto LXXXIII

Sri Rama abandons the proposal of performing Rajasiiya
sacrifice at the instance of Bharata
content: Rama, having finished his duties, informed Bharata and Lakshmana of his intention to perform the Rajasuya sacrifice.  He embraced them upon their arrival and explained his reasoning, stating his belief that the principles of righteousness are eternal and that discussing them eliminates wrongdoings.  Rama expressed his desire to perform the Rajasuya with his brothers, citing the examples of Mitra and Soma, who achieved elevated status through this sacrifice.  He then asked his brothers for their counsel on the matter, urging them to consider what would be most beneficial for the present and the future.  Bharata responded by praising Rama's virtue, strength, and fame, declaring that all kings revere him as a divine figure and the protector of the world.

summary:
Canto LXXXIII

Sri Rama abandons the proposal of performing Rajasiiya
sacrifice at the instance of Bharata
content: Bharata cautioned Rama against performing the Rajasuya sacrifice, arguing that it would lead to the destruction of royal families and valiant men. He emphasized that Rama's strength and authority already extended over the earth, making such a display of power unnecessary and potentially harmful.  Pleased with Bharata's wise counsel, Rama expressed his satisfaction and gratitude. He acknowledged the righteousness and wisdom in Bharata's words, particularly regarding the protection of the earth.  Consequently, Rama decided to abandon the idea of the Rajasuya sacrifice, recognizing that actions causing suffering should be avoided. He commended Bharata for his insightful advice, highlighting the importance of heeding good counsel even from younger individuals.

summary:
Canto LXXXIV

Laksmana advises Sri Rama to perform Aévamedha sacrifice and
narrates the story of Indra and Vrtra. Vrtra’s penance
and Indra’s appeal to Visnu to kill him
content: Vrtra, a powerful and respected demon, ruled the earth wisely and compassionately. His reign brought prosperity, with the earth yielding abundant fruits and flowers without cultivation.  Desiring the highest form of welfare, Vrtra decided to perform an arduous penance, leaving his eldest son, Madhuresvara, in charge.  Vrtra's intense penance alarmed Indra, who then sought Vishnu's help, confessing his inability to subdue the powerful and righteous demon.

summary:
Canto LXXXIV

Laksmana advises Sri Rama to perform Aévamedha sacrifice and
narrates the story of Indra and Vrtra. Vrtra’s penance
and Indra’s appeal to Visnu to kill him
content: Indra continued his appeal to Vishnu, expressing concern that if Vrtra continued his penance, he would gain dominion over all beings.  He emphasized Vishnu's power and urged him not to overlook the potential threat. Indra pointed out that Vrtra's current supremacy was a result of Vishnu's past favor and pleaded for Vishnu to consider the welfare of the world by intervening. The gods, Indra explained, looked to Vishnu for help in defeating Vrtra, reminding him of his past protection of the gods and highlighting the unbearable nature of Vrtra's power for others.  Indra concluded by positioning Vishnu as the only refuge for those without protection.

summary:
Canto LXXXV
Visnu’s lustre enters Indra’s body and his thunderbolt, Indra kills
Vrtra with his thunderbolt and is guilty of killing a
Brahmana and as a consequence has to go
to the region of darkness
content: Lakshmana, at Rama's request, recounts the tale of Vrtra's demise.  Vishnu, addressing the gods led by Indra, explains his reluctance to slay Vrtra due to past affection. However, recognizing the necessity of ensuring the gods' well-being, Vishnu reveals a plan: he will divide himself into three parts. One part will empower Indra, another will infuse Indra's thunderbolt, and the third will merge with the earth. This strategy will enable Indra to defeat Vrtra. Reassured by Vishnu's plan, the gods express their confidence and depart for the forest where Vrtra resides, seeking his destruction. Upon encountering Vrtra, the gods are awed and intimidated by his immense power and aura, questioning their ability to overcome him.

summary:
Canto LXXXV
Visnu’s lustre enters Indra’s body and his thunderbolt, Indra kills
Vrtra with his thunderbolt and is guilty of killing a
Brahmana and as a consequence has to go
to the region of darkness
content: Indra, wielding his thunderbolt, struck down Vrtra.  The impact of the mighty blow, which resembled a destructive fire, caused Vrtra's head to fall, terrifying the universe.  Indra, though victorious, was filled with remorse for killing Vrtra, a Brahmin. He fled to the ends of the earth, pursued by the guilt of his action.  The gods, led by Agni, found themselves without their leader and turned to Vishnu for guidance. They praised Vishnu as the creator and protector, acknowledging his role in Vrtra's demise and beseeching him to absolve Indra of his guilt. Vishnu reassured the gods, instructing them to have Indra perform the Asvamedha sacrifice to purify himself and regain his position.  Having offered this solution, Vishnu returned to his celestial abode, lauded by the gods.

Indra, after slaying the formidable Vrtra, became burdened with the sin of Brahmahatya (killing a Brahmin).  He lost consciousness and wandered aimlessly.  His absence caused turmoil in the world: the earth dried up, rivers and lakes disappeared, and all living beings suffered from drought.  The gods, witnessing the devastation, decided to perform the Asvamedha sacrifice, as advised by Vishnu.  They found Indra and, with him as their leader, commenced the grand ritual to purify him.  As the Asvamedha concluded, the personified Brahmahatya appeared before the gods and asked where she should reside.  The gods instructed her to divide herself into four parts. Brahmahatya agreed and declared that one part would inhabit overflowing rivers during the four months of the rainy season, while another part would forever remain on earth.

The personified Brahmahatya declared that her third part would reside in proud, young women for three nights each month, diminishing their pride.  Her fourth part would afflict those who falsely accused and killed innocent Brahmins. The gods accepted her terms.  Relieved and pleased, they then honored Indra, now free from the sin of Brahmahatya. With Indra restored, peace returned to the world.  Indra then performed the magnificent Asvamedha sacrifice.  The narrator then addresses Rama, advising him to perform the Asvamedha sacrifice, highlighting its power and benefits.  Rama, upon hearing Lakshmana’s wise and pleasing words, was deeply satisfied.

summary:
Canto LXXXVII
Sri Rama narrates to Laksmana the story of King Ila
content: King Rama, a skilled orator, smiled as he began to tell Lakshmana the story of King Ila. He confirmed Lakshmana's understanding of the Vrtra's slaying and the horse-sacrifice.  Rama described Ila, the virtuous ruler of Bahlika and son of Prajapati Kardama. King Ila, a renowned and powerful ruler, governed his kingdom with paternal care after conquering the earth. He was consistently honored by gods, demons, Nagas, Rakshasas, Gandharvas, and Yaksas, all of whom feared his wrath. The three worlds trembled when he was angered. Despite his power and righteousness, King Ila was known for his generous heart. In the pleasant month of Caitra, he embarked on a hunt with his army and retinue.  Though he killed countless animals, he remained unsatisfied. His hunt led him to the birthplace of Kartikeya, where Shiva, accompanied by his attendants, was with Parvati.  Wishing to please Parvati, Shiva took the form of a woman near a mountain stream. As a result, all masculine beings in the forest, including trees with masculine names, were transformed into female forms.

summary:
Canto LXXXVII
Sri Rama narrates to Laksmana the story of King Ila
content: King Ila, son of Kardama, arrived at Kartikeya's birthplace after hunting numerous animals.  He discovered that everything in the forest, including himself and his followers, had been transformed into female forms. Distressed by this, he realized Shiva was responsible and sought refuge in the god. Shiva, accompanied by Parvati, offered Ila a boon, excluding the return of his permanent manhood.  Ila, now female, appealed to Parvati.  Parvati, understanding Ila’s plight, granted him a compromise, allowing him to alternate monthly between male and female forms. Relieved, Ila accepted the boon, requesting to be a man for one month and a woman for the next.  Parvati assured him that in each form, he would have no memory of the other. Thus, Ila began his cyclical existence, alternating between King Ila and the beautiful lady, Ila.

summary:
Canto LXXXVIII
lla and Budha see each other, Budha gives the name Kirnpurusis

to her and her companions and asks them to take
their abode on the foot of the mountain
content: Laksmana and Bharata were awestruck by Rama's story of Ila.  They asked him to elaborate on Ila's experiences as a woman and later as a man.
